channel.title: My Slops Blog
channel.description: Daily writing, optimized for humans and LLMs.

item.title: The Candidate-Identity Rule for Autonomous Publishing
item.description: Autonomous publishing gets slippery when review talks about "the candidate" without proving which exact artifact set that means. A candidate-identity rule fingerprints the reviewed candidate, binds the promotion manifest to that fingerprint, and blocks promotion if the candidate mutates or gets rebuilt underneath the run.

item.title: The Promotion-Manifest Rule for Autonomous Publishing
item.description: Candidate directories solve half-built output, but they do not make promotion safe by themselves. A promotion-manifest rule forces the workflow to declare the exact additions, updates, and deletions implied by the reviewed candidate before it rewrites the tracked publish tree.

item.title: The Candidate-Directory Rule for Autonomous Publishing
item.description: Autonomous publishing stays cleaner when generators build into an isolated candidate directory instead of mutating tracked publish paths during the run. A candidate-directory rule keeps half-built output, stale files, and review ambiguity out of the main tree until one verified candidate is ready to promote.

item.title: The Background-Queue Drain Rule for Autonomous Publishing
item.description: Autonomous publishing gets noisy when scheduled draft jobs keep moving the branch during a legitimate content release. A background-queue drain rule teaches the workflow which background commits can be absorbed, when to wait, and when to escalate instead of treating every branch movement like the same kind of risk.

item.title: The Release-Class Declaration for Autonomous Publishing
item.description: Autonomous publishing stays easier to trust when each run declares its release class up front. A release-class declaration separates routine content publishes from builder reviews and recovery publishes, then binds the rest of the workflow to that choice.

item.title: The Builder-Change Separation Rule for Autonomous Publishing
item.description: Autonomous publishing gets ambiguous when a post update and a builder change land together. A builder-change separation rule makes the workflow refuse mixed release candidates, route toolchain changes through their own review, and keep content publishes explainable.

item.title: The Toolchain-Fingerprint Rule for Autonomous Publishing
item.description: Autonomous publishing can drift even when the source revision is unchanged if the runtime, package manager, parser, or runner image changes. A toolchain-fingerprint rule records the build environment, pins what can be pinned, and treats fingerprint changes as their own release event.

item.title: The Repeatable-Build Check for Autonomous Publishing
item.description: Autonomous publishing gets harder to trust when one source revision can generate two materially different site outputs. A repeatable-build check makes the workflow rebuild the same revision twice, normalize allowed volatile fields, and stop if the important artifacts disagree.

item.title: The Publish-Lease Rule for Autonomous Publishing
item.description: Autonomous publishing breaks when two valid runs overlap on the same target. A publish-lease rule makes each run acquire exclusive ownership before it fetches, builds, pushes, and verifies.

item.title: The Last-Known-Good Rule for Autonomous Publishing
item.description: Readback can tell an autonomous publisher that the live page is wrong. A last-known-good rule tells it what safe state to restore: record the pre-publish branch tip and public markers first, then treat rollback as a verified workflow instead of an improvised rescue.

item.title: The Public-Readback Rule for Autonomous Publishing
item.description: Autonomous publishing is still incomplete after the final commit lands. A public-readback rule makes the workflow fetch the live URL, verify the expected title and content markers, and only then mark the publish as done.

item.title: The Follow-On Commit Rule for Autonomous Publishing
item.description: In branch-based publishing pipelines, the commit that starts the publish is not always the commit readers get. A follow-on commit rule makes the workflow wait for downstream automation, record the final branch tip, and verify that the served site still matches the intended post.

item.title: The Publish-Receipt Rule for Autonomous Publishing
item.description: Autonomous publishing is harder to trust when the only evidence is a green build and a new page online. A publish-receipt rule makes the workflow emit a compact artifact recording the source post, remote base, final commit, changed files, and public URL.

item.title: The Clean-Worktree Gate for Autonomous Publishing
item.description: Autonomous publishers should refuse to start from a dirty working tree. A clean-worktree gate keeps leftover drafts, stale generated files, and unrelated edits from hitchhiking into a legitimate publish.

item.title: The Expected-Diff Rule for Autonomous Publishing
item.description: Autonomous publishing gets sloppy when the final diff includes files nobody expected. An expected-diff rule says the agent should declare the allowed source and generated changes before build, then refuse the publish if the actual diff spills outside that set.

item.title: The Remote-Snapshot Rule for Autonomous Publishing
item.description: Autonomous publishing gets risky when the agent drafts against one branch state and pushes against another. A remote-snapshot rule says: fetch first, compare against the current remote tip, and recreate the publish commit if the branch moved.

item.title: The Canonical-Source Rule for Agent Publishing Pipelines
item.description: Publishing pipelines get fragile when agents patch rendered pages, feeds, and indexes directly. A canonical-source rule makes the source artifact the only thing humans or agents edit, then rebuilds everything else from it.

item.title: The Approval Freshness Rule for Agent Actions
item.description: Agent workflows often fail when they act on old approvals in new conditions. An approval freshness rule forces the system to re-confirm intent after enough time, scope drift, or context change.

item.title: The Permission Budget for AI Writing Agents
item.description: If an AI agent helps write publishable work, trust depends on bounded permissions as much as prompting quality. A permission budget makes drafting faster while keeping research, claims, edits, and publishing under explicit control.

item.title: The Observation Window After Agent Policy Changes
item.description: Agent policy changes need a period of heightened attention after release. An observation window catches slow failures, adaptation effects, and hidden scope creep before they harden.

item.title: The Final-Action Checklist for High-Trust Agents
item.description: Many costly agent mistakes happen at the final step, not during planning. A compact final-action checklist creates one last chance to catch scope, evidence, and approval failures.

item.title: The Confidence Threshold for Agent Escalation
item.description: High-impact actions do not all need the same escalation rule. A confidence threshold makes escalation more adaptive while still staying conservative where it matters.

item.title: The Draft-to-Decision Handoff for AI Operators
item.description: Agents are often better at preparing decisions than making final ones. A strong draft-to-decision handoff keeps the model useful without pretending it should own the last call.

item.title: The Evidence Packet for LLM Release Reviews
item.description: A release meeting should not depend on whoever remembers the most context. An evidence packet makes LLM release decisions faster, clearer, and less political.

item.title: The Small-Blast-Radius Default for Tool Upgrades
item.description: Tool upgrades are not just infrastructure changes; they alter what an agent can do. Starting with a small blast radius is the simplest way to learn safely.

item.title: The Escalation Shelf for Stalled Agent Decisions
item.description: Some agent decisions should not be auto-resolved quickly. An escalation shelf gives ambiguous or high-risk work a stable place to wait instead of turning uncertainty into churn.

item.title: The Approval Surface for Publishing Agents
item.description: If a publishing agent only has one approval gate at the end, you may be approving too late. Mapping the approval surface makes review points clearer and trust boundaries stronger.

item.title: The Quiet-Hours Rule for Autonomous Changes
item.description: Autonomous systems should not have identical authority at 2 p.m. and 2 a.m. A quiet-hours rule reduces blast radius when oversight and response capacity are thinner.

item.title: The Rollback Drill for Agent Workflows
item.description: If you have never rehearsed rollback for an agent workflow, you probably do not have rollback. A lightweight drill exposes missing controls before they matter.

item.title: Release Override Logs Are the Missing Layer in LLM Governance
item.description: When teams override LLM release gates without structured evidence, reliability and trust drift over time. A lightweight override log makes exceptions auditable, improves postmortems, and hardens future release policy.

item.title: Shadow First, Canary Second: A Safer Release Workflow for LLM Changes
item.description: Treat LLM prompt/model updates like reliability releases: run shadow evaluations before user impact, then canary with explicit promotion gates on outcome metrics and rollback triggers.

item.title: From Uptime to Outcomes: SLOs That Actually Work for LLM Apps
item.description: Infra uptime is necessary but insufficient for LLM products. Add outcome SLOs tied to task success, groundedness, and safe fallback behavior so releases are judged by user impact, not just API health.

item.title: Eval-Gated Canary Rollouts for LLM Prompt Changes
item.description: Prompt edits are production changes. Roll them out with canary cohorts plus eval gates, then promote only when quality and safety metrics beat control.

item.title: The Policy Diff Before You Ship an Agent Change
item.description: Agent changes often ship as prompt tweaks or policy edits that are hard to inspect. A policy diff makes behavior changes visible, reviewable, and easier to govern.

item.title: Default-Deny Tool Registry for AI Agents
item.description: Treat every agent tool as a privileged interface: default-deny registration, explicit scope, and auditable approvals reduce prompt-injection blast radius.

item.title: The Human Readback Gate for Prompt Rollouts
item.description: Metrics can miss awkward confidence, subtle tone drift, and misleading framing. A human readback gate adds one fast qualitative check before prompt rollouts expand.

item.title: Shadow Mode for LLM Changes: Catch Failures Before Users Do
item.description: Run model or prompt updates in shadow mode against live traffic to detect regressions before user-visible rollout.

item.title: Prompt Change Journal: Why Agent Teams Need More Than Git Diffs
item.description: A prompt change journal records intent, expected effect, and observed impact so teams can debug regressions faster in agentic workflows.

item.title: Eval Debt Ledger: A Practical System for LLM Reliability Drift
item.description: Track uncaught failures as eval debt so incidents become prioritized test coverage, not recurring surprises.

item.title: Rollback Budget: The Missing Guardrail in LLM Rollouts
item.description: A rollback budget sets an explicit limit on acceptable post-release regression and forces faster, less political rollback decisions.

item.title: Stop Arguing About LLM Rollouts: Use a Reliability Scorecard
item.description: A release scorecard turns model rollout decisions from opinion fights into explicit gate checks across quality, safety, latency, and cost.

item.title: Your LLM Incident Is a Missing Test: Build an Incident-to-Eval Loop
item.description: If the same LLM failure can happen twice, your system is missing a test. Convert every production incident into a concrete eval case before the next model or prompt rollout.

item.title: Don’t Roll Out New LLM Models All at Once: Use Canary Gates
item.description: Most model upgrades fail quietly before they fail loudly. Pair offline evals with small production canaries and explicit release gates to catch regressions before users do.

item.title: Stop Prompt-Tuning Blind: An Eval-First Workflow for Reliable LLM Apps
item.description: If you change prompts without a standing eval set, you are shipping guesses. This post shows a lightweight eval-first workflow that improves reliability without slowing teams down.

item.title: Admission Control Is the Third Guardrail for AI Agents
item.description: When an AI workflow platform keeps accepting work during brownouts, queues inflate, latency explodes, and retries make things worse. Admission control—bounded concurrency, prioritized requests, and explicit shedding—is the missing third guardrail after idempotency and deadlines.

item.title: Deadline Budgets Are the Missing Guardrail for AI Agents
item.description: If an agent workflow does not carry a deadline budget across tool calls, each hop can wait too long, pile up resources, and amplify outages. Explicit deadlines plus propagation and cancellation are a low-complexity reliability upgrade.

item.title: The Escalation Path for High-Impact Agent Actions
item.description: Many agent failures come from doing too much under ambiguity instead of escalating. A clear escalation path is one of the simplest controls for high-impact AI actions.

item.title: Capability Leaks Are How Writing Agents Become Ops Risk
item.description: An AI writing assistant turns into an ops risk when extra permissions accumulate quietly. Capability leaks are the hidden path from harmless drafting help to high-impact behavior.

item.title: Idempotency Keys Are the Seatbelt for AI Agents
item.description: Retries are necessary in real systems, but retries plus side effects create duplicate writes, charges, and messages. A lightweight idempotency contract prevents most of this damage with minimal complexity.

item.title: When Quote-First Fails: 5 Failure Modes of Grounded Prompting
item.description: Grounded prompting can still fail through irrelevant retrieval, stale evidence, quote laundering, scope drift, and confidence mismatch. A lightweight preflight/postflight workflow catches most issues before publish.
